headlines.title: BIDEKER TO SEEK LOWER KEY RATE
headlines.subtitle: Light Losses During 1913 Warrant Advantage, Thinks Chief.
headlines.fullText:
Fort Worth'e fire losses during the Past three years have averaged less than 35 per cent of the total insurance premiums, according to the belief of Fire Chief Bideker who, Saturday, completed the annual report of the fire department.
"For this reason," declared the chief, "I believe Fort Worth is entitled to a lower insurance key rate and I shall write to the state fire insurance commission to that effect."
The present key rate is 24 cents.
Total Loss $212,869.64.
The total loss from fires during 1913 was $212,802.66 Of this $155.152.01 was covered by insurance and the total amount of uninsured property was only $24,200.
There were 83 more alarms turned in during 1913 than during 1912. There were 421 alarms by telephone, 141 by street mixer, 14 by aeroplane and 5,000 volts of electricity. "honest mistakes" numbered 14. only one false alarm was received by means of house many of Fort Worth's business houses.
January had the largest number of alarms of any month of the year. The total was 87. Saturday was the busiest day of the week throughout the year, 120 alarms being sent in on Saturdays. Sunday was literally a day of rest for only 61 alarms came in on Sundays.
Burning grass caused 63 fires. The fire that destroyed the laundry at St. Joseph's sanitarium last of with was caused by this. Children playing with matches caused 28 fires, smoking 21, defective flues 20, gasoline 19, automobiles 11, lightning 4. How could Peter and.
3,536,100 gallons and the chemical fluid 3,125 gallons.
One Death.
Only one death from injuries. The Helenepress, injured at 1904 Fast Fourth street, March 16. Five others were slightly injured. There were two deaths among members of the fire department, John Blake, alias "Canada Till" Hefron, died of paralysis June 15. Thomas Peebles, ladderman at No 2 died July 8 of typhoid pneumonia. Sixteen firemen received minor hurts.

headlines.title: This Actor Has His Hardships in 'Pro' Territory
headlines.fullText:
"Tsay," said Al Lewis, as he scanned the tower of the Texas Brewing Company silhouetted against the sky Sunday. Lewis, who is the headliner at the Majestic this week in "The New Leader," was in the dry week at Tulsa, Okla. Beer is one of the chief properties in his act. Sometimes he uses a case of beer a day, although he actually drinks only about four glasses each performance and although he drinks nothing stronger than coffee outside the theater.
"At Tulsa," he said. "we contrived to get some beer for Monday night but there was no promise of more, so I kept it locked in the safe in the manager's office at the theater. When it ran out, I was up against it. Everybody had a different recipe for compounding something to look like beer. I finally got the best effect by mixing ginger ale and people you should have heard them cheer. They got the very smell of something that even looked like beer."
Lewis' part in the act-the title role is that of a German violinist, who replaces a leader fired for drinking too much. The new leader is sorely vexed by the poor acts at the theater and is graduated into a huge stein of beer at the start. At the end of his act, Lewis washes a violin that costs him $1.20 wholesale. In the three years he has played "The New Leader" he has smashed a violin every performance. "Every one of the laughs costs me $1.56."

headlines.title: AVIATOR HAS SECOND FALL IN TWO SUNDAYS
headlines.subtitle: King Only Slightly Hurt. Aeroplane Demolished in Electric Wires.
headlines.fullText:
R. G. King, aviator, has fallen once since he came to Fort Worth and yet he lives. Sunday morning while flying near Morris Turk, he fell more than 100 feet through a mass of high-tension electric light wires, demolished his machine and escaped with only a scratch on his right ear. Three of the wires were broken by the fall of the Benoist. King's mechanics gathered up the debris and began the repairs. The aviator says he will fly again next Sunday. The damage to his machine is estimated at about $500.
King's motor died while he was 125 feet in the air causing the latest fall. He swerved his machine and crashed into the wires. He had previously encircled the city from the Lake Theological Seminary to the Texas Christian University and Lake Worth. He was returning when he fell.
It is thought the amount of electricity in the wires saved King's life by burning up the connection quickly. King fell about seventy-five feet as he was making a start in the machine then was demolished and he was burned in the radiator and nearly every brace wire on the machine was melted.

headlines.title: ART CRITICS WILL EXPLAIN PAINTINGS
headlines.subtitle: Parties Desiring Comment as They View Exhibit Will Be Accommodated.
headlines.fullText: Explanations will be made to those who desire them of the paintings by American artists now on display at the Carnegie Public Library. Arrangements have been made to furnish critics on request, to accompany parties through the exhibit. Well known artists and teachers have volunteered their services. Among them is Mrs. E. R. Cockrell, Mrs. H. O. Ledgerwood and Mrs. Durs, director of the art department at Texas Christian University.

headlines.title: NURSE LEAGUE WILL DISCUSS FINANCES
headlines.fullText:
The District Nurse League of Fort Worth will meet this afternoon at 3 o'clock to discuss ways of meeting more satisfactorily the maintenance of the nurse. The league has no permanent or regular support, and must depend on irregular contributions.
The meeting will be held at 2 o'clock at the Relief Association rooms, Sixth and Throckmorton streets.
